title: Three-flavor supernova neutrino simulation using a hybrid quantum-classical algorithm with qutrits
authors: Daniel J. Heimsoth, A. Baha Balantekin, Pooja Siwach
abstract: We simulate a self-interacting three-flavor neutrino system within a core-collapse supernova using a hybrid classical-quantum algorithm on a qutrit computer. Based on the Dirac-Frenkel evolution equations, we employ a variation of the quantum-assisted simulator (QAS) to calculate the system's time evolution operator by performing qutrit Hadamard tests to find expectation values of unitary operators in the Hamiltonian. The time evolution simulation is then done classically. We find that the hybrid algorithm produces results comparable to an exact numerical integration out to times of $t \approx 30 \,ω_0^{-1}$ with time step $δt = 0.005 \,ω_0^{-1}$, where $ω_0$ is the energy scale of the single neutrino vacuum oscillations. We discuss the lessons learned in simulating neutrino systems using this hybrid quantum-classical algorithm, along with the advantages it offers over quantum Trotterization.
summary: This paper presents a hybrid quantum-classical algorithm using qutrits to simulate three-flavor neutrino oscillations in supernovae. By using the Dirac-Frenkel variational principle, the method avoids the deep circuits required by Trotterization and achieves accuracy comparable to exact numerical integration for significant time intervals. This approach provides a potential pathway for simulating complex many-body neutrino physics on near-term quantum hardware.
main_problem: Simulating the complex time evolution of a self-interacting, three-flavor neutrino system in a core-collapse supernova while overcoming the high error rates and deep circuit requirements of traditional quantum Trotterization on NISQ devices.
main_result: The hybrid quantum-classical algorithm produces results comparable to exact numerical integration for up to t approximately 20-30 omega_0^-1, avoiding the need for deep, error-prone Trotter circuits.
method: A variation of the Quantum-Assisted Simulator (QAS) based on the Dirac-Frenkel variational principle, using qutrit Hadamard tests to calculate expectation values of unitary operators, with the actual time evolution performed classically.
model_or_system: A three-flavor (SU(3)) neutrino system in a core-collapse supernova environment, modeled using a neutrino bulb model and a single-angle approximation, with a Hamiltonian containing vacuum and neutrino-neutriino interaction terms.
key_observables: Survival probability of the initial state, mass state probabilities (P_m1, P_m2, P_m3), von Neumann entropy, and expectation values of Q3 and Q8.
important_parameters: Energy scale of vacuum oscillations (omega_0), time step (delta t = 0.005 omega_0^-1), and number of measurement shots (2,000,000 per test).
main_assumptions: Single-angle approximation, neutrinos travel at the speed of light, and neutrinos are treated in a discrete momentum set.
figures_summary: Circuit diagrams for qutrit Hadamard tests; comparison of survival probability and mass state probabilities between the hybrid algorithm and exact ODE; comparison of asymptotic expectation values and von Neumann entropy; and error analysis using the alpha-norm.
paper_structure: The paper identifies the limitations of Trotterization, proposes a qutrit-based SU(3) mapping using the Dirac-Frenkel principle, details the hybrid algorithm implementation, presents simulation results and error analysis, and concludes with scaling challenges and future directions.
why_interesting: It demonstrates a practical application of qutrit-based quantum computing and variational algorithms to simulate many-body flavor dynamics, offering an alternative to standard qubit-based Trotterization for complex SU(3) systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 3. Survival probability of an initial |eeµτ⟩state, calculated with the Dirac-Frenkel quantum-assisted simulator (median in black and total range in blue) and a numerical integration (dotted red). Each Hadamard test in the hybrid simulator used nshots = 2000000 shots, and the simulation was repeated nruns = 65 times. At late times, the hybrid algorithm loses any resemblance to the exact calculation.

FIG. 4. Mass state probability of each particle from an initial |eeµτ⟩state, calculated with the Dirac-Frenkel quantum- assisted simulator (solid lines) and a numerical integration (dotted lines). Data from the same run as in Fig. 3.

FIG. 5. Final asymptotic Q3 and Q8 expectation val- ues of the initial |eeµτ⟩state, calculated with the Dirac- Frenkel quantum-assisted simulator (stars) and a classi- cal numerical integration (triangles). The initial values for pure flavor states are shown in blue squares.

FIG. 6. Von Neumann entropy of each particle from an initial |eeµτ⟩state, calculated with the Dirac-Frenkel quantum-assisted simulator (solid lines) and a classical numerical integration (dotted lines). Data from the same run as in Fig. 3.
FIG. 7. Error in the norm ⃗α† clE⃗α as a function of inte- gration time and relative matrix error compared to the classically calculated matrices.
